dateStatements.label: separately issued introduction and completed bound publication

entities.label: Thomas Wright's 1845–1846 edition of The Seven Sages
notes: The 1845 issue title identifies Percy Society publication no. 53 and January 1845. Folger records that the introduction was issued separately in 1846 as no. 64.
Wright states that he corrected some scribal errors sparingly, restored a detached n to the preceding article, and separated words that the manuscript joined.
Printed notes on pp. 117–118 document further readings, supplied letters, defective lines, and emendations; those notes are not part of the U-M encoding.

rights.basis: The London edition appeared in 1845–1846. U.S. Copyright Office Circular 15A describes a maximum 95-year term for works already under statutory protection before 1978 and notes the separate restoration rules for some foreign works; that maximum term has elapsed. U-M independently marks this item No Copyright–United States.
rights.component: 1845–1846 printed text, introduction, notes, and typographical arrangement

rights.basis: Thomas Wright died in 1877; current UK guidance gives written works a usual term of life plus 70 years and published typographical arrangements 25 years from publication.
rights.component: Thomas Wright's editorial text and the edition's typographical arrangement

holding.notes: The Seven Sages component is now in vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb. The 1856 catalogue locates the same component at legacy manuscript pp. 594–612. entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17
notes: This is the sole surviving witness of the Midland Version identified by current scholarship.

The witness date, England production, Midland language association, physical extent, and image terms remain witness-layer facts and are not transferred wholesale to the abstract Seven Sages tradition.

physicalDescription.extent: Current description: vol. 1, 261 folios; vol. 2, 93 folios; vol. 3, 87 folios. Legacy 1856 description: 424 leaves, paginated 1–848.
physicalDescription.format: large folio manuscript, now divided into three volumes
notes: The Seven Sages occupies vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb, nineteen manuscript pages in three columns of about sixty lines each.
The 1856 catalogue calls this portion carelessly transcribed in a running hand of the second half of the fourteenth century.
The modern database records no illustrations and twenty-four other texts in the three-volume miscellany, including Piers Plowman and Mandeville's Travels immediately before this work in vol. 3.

lineage.summary: The checked-in CME00018 XML represents the University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item keyed from Thomas Wright's edition The Seven Sages, in English Verse. Wright's poem text appeared as Percy Society publication no. 53 in January 1845; a separately issued introduction and notes followed in 1846 and were bound with it. Wright transcribed the anonymous Middle English Midland Version from Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17, now vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb (legacy manuscript pp. 594–612). The witness is a shared parchment miscellany, not a work-exclusive codex. A complete public scan of the bound printed object and a complete scan of Cambridge's 1856 catalogue volume are cached locally with exact work portions. No publicly obtainable complete manuscript facsimile was verified on 2026-07-11; onsite consultation and mediated Cambridge imaging remain the documented routes.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous
catalogSummary.date: Composed 1350–1400; attested by a late-fourteenth-century manuscript and first known in print in Wright's 1845 text issue
catalogSummary.region: Middle English work with an English Midlands dialect association; exact place of composition unknown; frame narrative set in Rome
catalogSummary.title: The Seven Sages of Rome (Midland Version)

catalogingSubject.label: The Seven Sages of Rome (Midland Version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the anonymous Middle English verse Midland Version, Text D, of The Seven Sages of Rome, composed in the second half of the fourteenth century and uniquely witnessed in Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17, vol. 3, ff. 54ra–63rb. It excludes the wider multilingual Seven Sages / Book of Sindbad tradition, other Middle English redactions, the full manuscript miscellany, Thomas Wright's 1845–1846 editorial apparatus, Jill Whitelock's 2005 edition, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, and digital scans as separate layers.

contentParts.label: Opening frame narrative
notes: The part boundary is the XML/edition boundary before the first DIV2 and does not claim that the manuscript supplied a formal heading at this point.
contentParts.summary: The emperor, prince, sages, education, remarriage, astrological danger, return to court, accusation, and first threatened execution through the first demand for a delaying tale.

contentParts.label: Fifteen tale divisions and frame conclusion
notes: Successive divisions share printed boundary pages because a new tale can begin partway through a page.
The generic DIV2 heads are inherited from Wright's printed layout and do not name most stories.
contentParts.summary: Fourteen alternating Empress/Sage tales, the prince's final tale of the ravens, and the frame transitions and conclusion carried within the fifteen printed tale divisions.

assertion.notes: This is the scholarly date of the Midland redaction, not the earlier 1275–1320 range assigned to the broader Middle English Version A tradition.

dateStatements.display: The Midland Version is dated by current Whitelock-based scholarship to 1350–1400

assertion.notes: This dates the witness and establishes attestation; it does not independently prove an exact composition year.

dateStatements.display: The sole surviving witness, Cambridge University Library MS Dd.1.17, is dated to the second half of the fourteenth century (1350–1400)

assertion.notes: The 1845 issue and its use of Dd.1.17 are direct. 'First known' remains qualified because the inspected sources do not present an exhaustive publication census.

dateStatements.display: First known printed as Percy Society publication no. 53 in January 1845, edited by Thomas Wright

extent.description: The complete XML poem has 3,455 L elements in 16 LG elements: one opening frame group and 15 DIV2 tale groups. It also has 16 HEAD elements, 117 PB elements spanning the title page and printed poem pp. 1–116, and 342 MILESTONE elements for printed line numbers. These are encoding and edition metrics; the 15 DIV2 units include frame transitions and should not be treated as fifteen freestanding authored works.

formStatements.description: A 3,455-line Middle English verse romance, predominantly in rhyming couplets; the XML represents the opening frame and fifteen tale divisions in sixteen LG elements.

assertion.notes: The prince's silence and threatened execution frame alternating cautionary tales by the Empress and sages, followed by the prince's own tale.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Modern English appears only in editorial and catalog apparatus and is not an intrinsic language of the medieval work.
The preferred title and 1350–1400 composition range describe the Midland redaction, not every Seven Sages version.
The witness's date, production in England, physical extent, and current holding are kept distinct from the work's composition and from Wright's publication place.
Wright's separately issued introduction and notes inform the catalog record but remain editorial apparatus outside the medieval work.
The XML's 3,455-line count and fifteen tale divisions are encoding metrics verified against the immediate edition; the generic printed tale headings are not normalized into manuscript rubrics.
The exact immediate source route before the Midland redactor and the availability of a complete manuscript facsimile remain explicit open questions.

notes: The lineage manifest records the full dated search scope and mediated Cambridge imaging route.

openQuestions.question: What keying method and correction history produced the U-M encoding, and which differences from Wright are intentional rather than transcription errors?

assertion.notes: The redaction belongs to the Middle English Version A tradition and its witness was produced in England. This does not establish an exact place of composition.
Midland is the established linguistic/redactional classification; it is not narrowed here to a modern county or settlement.

notes: Rome is the frame narrative's principal court setting; many embedded tales use other or generic settings.
Current scholarship treats the Midland Version as an anonymous redaction or adaptation. The exact immediate source—lost Middle English parent or French prose A—remains unresolved.

Wright's responsibility is for the immediate 1845–1846 source edition. He states that he corrected some scribal errors sparingly, restored a detached article n, and separated words joined in the manuscript.

subjects.label: False accusation, delayed execution, and rash judgment

assertion.notes: The classification describes a demonstrable thematic and rhetorical pattern; it does not collapse every embedded tale into one identical moral.

subjects.label: Education, astrological foreknowledge, and enforced silence

summaries.value: A Roman emperor entrusts his son to seven sages. When the prince returns, astrological danger requires him to remain silent; his stepmother propositions him, then falsely accuses him after he refuses her. The emperor repeatedly orders his son's execution, while the sages and Empress alternate cautionary tales until the prince can speak, expose the plot, and tell a final story of a child who understands ravens.

The XML presents 591 opening-frame lines followed by fifteen tale-labeled verse divisions: the fourteen alternating Empress/Sage stories Arbor, Canis, Aper, Medicus, Gaza, Puteus, Senescalcus, Tentamina, Virgilius, Avis, Sapientes, Vidua, Roma, and Inclusa, followed by the prince's tale of the ravens and the frame conclusion.

The fourteen-name specialist story list and the XML's fifteen DIV2 elements are compatible modeling conventions: the XML gives the prince's final ravens story its own tale division, while the database lists the alternating Empress/Sage cycle. Generic printed heads such as A TALE are editorial labels, not recovered medieval story titles.

notes: This is the established modern title for the cataloged redaction; it is not asserted as a manuscript rubric.

work.summary: A Roman emperor entrusts his son to seven sages. When the prince returns, astrological danger requires him to remain silent; his stepmother propositions him, then falsely accuses him after he refuses her. The emperor repeatedly orders his son's execution, while the sages and Empress alternate cautionary tales until the prince can speak, expose the plot, and tell a final story of a child who understands ravens.
